title: Style transfer in Gen AI and what can be done.

html:
Executive Summary

Achieving consistent visual style across diverse subjects and compositions remains one of the most challenging problems in AI-generated images. While consumer tools like Seedream and Nano Banana offer convenient quick-start solutions, they operate as black-box inference routers with limited precision. For production-grade style control, this guide covers tested approaches ranked by accuracy and implementation complexity.

Quick answer: A hybrid approach combining LoRA fine-tuning + IP-Adapter + ControlNet delivers 90–95% style fidelity with full creative control. This guide walks through the complete pipeline.

Part 1: Top-Tier Approaches Ranked by Accuracy

1. LoRA Fine-Tuning (Most Reliable) — 85–90% Fidelity

What It Is

Train a lightweight adapter (~50–150MB) on curated images of your target style. The LoRA learns distributed style patterns and blends seamlessly with base models.

Advantages

  • Consistency: Applies reliably across all subjects and prompts
  • Portability: Works with any FLUX 2 / SDXL checkpoint
  • Permanence: Baked into weights, always available
  • Cost-effective: One-time training cost (~$3–10 on RunPod)

Disadvantages

  • Requires 2–4 hours training time
  • Needs quality dataset curation and captioning
  • Cannot adjust style strength post-training
  • Prone to overfitting if dataset is too homogeneous

When to Use

  • Production pipelines with consistent aesthetic targets
  • Video generation (frame-to-frame consistency)
  • Multi-thousand image batches
  • When you own the style definition and want permanence

2. IP-Adapter + ControlNet Stack (Most Flexible) — 80–90% Fidelity

What It Is

Reference image → CLIP Vision encodes → IP-Adapter injects into model → ControlNet provides structural guidance

Reference Image (style)
    ↓ CLIP Vision Encode
    ↓ IP-Adapter (60-80% strength)
    ↓ ControlNet Depth/Canny (structural constraint)
    ↓ Output

Advantages

  • Non-destructive: Swap styles instantly at inference
  • Flexible: Test multiple reference images without retraining
  • Composable: Stack with LoRA, embeddings, other adapters
  • Fast iteration: Real-time style testing and refinement
  • Zero training cost: Use off-the-shelf models

Disadvantages

  • Requires reference image quality
  • Manual weight tuning needed (0.5–0.8 range)
  • More inference complexity
  • Less reliable than LoRA for very novel subjects

When to Use

  • Experimentation & style discovery
  • Multi-style projects (swap reference, same base)
  • Real-time creative direction changes
  • When you don’t want permanent style commitment

3. Hybrid: CLIP Embedding + Latent Conditioning (Most Powerful) — 90–95% Fidelity

What It Is

Extract CLIP vision embeddings from reference images, inject directly into diffusion latent space, combine with low-strength LoRA for stabilization.

Advantages

  • Extreme fidelity: Direct latent space control
  • Composable: Works with LoRA and other conditioners
  • Non-destructive: Style can be toggled or weighted
  • Precise: Controls distributed style patterns directly

Disadvantages

  • Requires custom ComfyUI nodes
  • Advanced setup (not beginner-friendly)
  • Limited documentation & community examples
  • Needs experimentation to dial in weights

When to Use

  • Studio-grade production requiring pixel-perfect consistency
  • When 90–95% fidelity is a hard requirement
  • Teams with technical depth to debug custom workflows

Part 2: Practical Implementation Roadmap

Quick Win: LoRA Training (2–4 hours)

For fast style establishment:

Step 1: Gather & Export Images

  • Collect 50–150 diverse images of your target aesthetic
  • Vary: subjects, environments, lighting, time of day, weather
  • The style of the image is much more important than the subject or composition. You can crop sections of high-quality images to maximize what you can teach the LoRA

Step 2: Dataset Organization

/project/style_lora/
├── images/
│   ├── 001_reference.png
│   ├── 002_reference.png
│   └── ... (50-150 total)
└── captions/  (auto-generate next)

Step 3: Auto-Caption & Review

  • Use BLIP2 or JoyCaption to generate captions
  • Critical: Auto-captions regularly include the subject’s distinctive features in the description text rather than isolating them to the trigger word. Always review and manually correct captions before training
  • For style LoRAs: describe visual properties, not style itself

Good caption:

"ornate architecture with red stone arches, warm amber glow, light filtering through carved screens, cinematic depth of field"

Bad caption:

"donghua animated style, 3D render aesthetic, artistic rendering"

Step 4: Train on FLUX 2

Using Kohya_SS (recommended):

git clone https://github.com/kohya-ss/sd-scripts.git
cd sd-scripts
pip install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
python kohya_gui.py --listen 0.0.0.0 --server_port 7860

Configuration for style LoRA:

pretrained_model: "flux2-pro-gguf-q8"
network_module: "networks.lora_flux"
network_dim: 64              # Larger for style (32 for character)
network_alpha: 32
learning_rate: 5e-5          # Lower for stability
lr_scheduler: "cosine_with_restarts"
lr_warmup_steps: 100
max_train_steps: 2500        # More steps than character LoRA
train_batch_size: 1
resolution: 1024
mixed_precision: "bf16"
optimizer_type: "AdamW8bit"
gradient_checkpointing: true
cache_latents: true
caption_extension: ".txt"

Key differences from character LoRA:

  • network_dim: 64 instead of 32 (style is distributed, needs capacity)
  • max_train_steps: 2500 instead of 2000 (style is subtle, needs iterations)
  • learning_rate: 5e-5 instead of 1e-4 (prevent over-learning)

Training timeline:

  • A100 (RunPod): 1–3 hours, ~$2–5
  • RTX 4090: 2–4 hours
  • 24GB consumer GPU: 4–8 hours

Output: style_lora.safetensors (~80-150MB)

Production Setup: LoRA + IP-Adapter + ControlNet

Once LoRA is trained, create a ComfyUI workflow combining all techniques:

Node Stack:

Load Checkpoint (FLUX 2)
  ↓ Load LoRA (your trained style)
  ↓ Apply LoRA (strength: 0.6-0.7)
  ↓ Load IP-Adapter
  ↓ Load Reference Image
  ↓ CLIP Vision Encode (reference)
  ↓ Apply IP-Adapter (strength: 0.5-0.6)
  ↓ Load ControlNet (Depth/Canny)
  ↓ Apply ControlNet (strength: 0.7)
  ↓ CLIP Text Encode (positive prompt)
  ↓ CLIP Text Encode (negative prompt)
  ↓ KSampler (30 steps, cfg: 7.5)
  ↓ VAE Decode
  ↓ Save

Strength Guidelines:

  • LoRA: 0.6–0.8 (stronger = more pronounced style)
  • IP-Adapter: 0.5–0.7 (moderate reference influence)
  • ControlNet: 0.6–0.8 (composition lock)

Bleeding Edge: CLIP Embedding + Latent Conditioning

For 95%+ fidelity projects:

Step 1: Extract embedding

Load Reference Image
  → CLIP Vision Encode
  → Save Embedding

Step 2: Inject into diffusion

Base Model
  ↓ Embed Conditioning Node (custom)
  ↓ KSampler
  → Output

Requires:

  • Custom ComfyUI nodes (community-developed, not official)
  • Experimentation with embedding weights (0.3–1.0)
  • Testing across diverse prompts

Result: Direct style control in latent space, preserving composition and subject.

Part 3: Comparison Matrix

Approach Setup Inference Cost Flexibility Fidelity Debugging Best For LoRA Only 2–4 hrs training $0 Medium (fixed) 85–90% Easy Production, consistency IP-Adapter Only 5 min + models $0 Very High 75–85% Medium Quick tests, iteration LoRA + IP-Adapter 2–4 hrs + 10 min $0 Very High 90–95% Medium Recommended hybrid CLIP Embedding Complex setup $0 High 90–95% Hard Studio-grade, precision Multi-Model Chain ~1 hr setup High High 90–95% Hard Final polish, hero shots Consumer Tools (Seedream) 0 min Pay-per-use Low 60–70% N/A Baseline only

Part 4: Production Workflow for Large-Scale Generation

Batch Processing Pipeline

# Pseudocode for production batch
style_config = {
    "base_model": "FLUX-2-Pro",
    "lora_file": "style_lora.safetensors",
    "lora_strength": 0.7,
    "reference_image": "anchor_style.png",
    "ipadapter_strength": 0.6,
    "controlnet": "depth",
    "controlnet_strength": 0.7,
}
for batch in batches:
    for prompt in batch.prompts:
        image = generate(
            config=style_config,
            prompt=prompt,
            steps=30,
            cfg=7.5,
            seed=batch.seed
        )
        validate_consistency(image, reference=style_config["reference_image"])
        save(image, batch.output_dir)

Quality Assurance Checklist

  • [ ] Color grading matches reference (check HSL values)
  • [ ] Lighting style consistent (shadows, highlights, ambient)
  • [ ] Texture characteristics preserved (photorealistic, painterly, etc.)
  • [ ] Works across different subjects (characters, environments, objects)
  • [ ] Composition not over-constrained (ControlNet strength appropriate)
  • [ ] No artifacts or style leakage

Infrastructure Requirements (Optional)

For large batches (10K+ images):

Compute:

  • A100 on Azure AKS or RunPod cluster
  • KEDA autoscaling for variable load
  • Redis queue for job management

Storage:

  • Model cache (ComfyUI models): ~50GB
  • Batch outputs: Scale with requirements
  • Reference images: <1GB

Cost estimation:

  • Per-image: $0.01–0.05 (inference only, no training)
  • Batch of 10,000: $100–500

Part 5: Best Practices & Recommendations

Dataset Curation

  1. Diversity is critical: Make sure to have all sort of images such as night time, day time, cloudy, etc.
  2. Aim for 50–150 images; quality > quantity (20 excellent images beat 200 mediocre)
  3. Vary: subject matter, composition, framing, lighting angles
  4. Crop high-quality images to extract style details (sky, lighting, textures)

Captioning Strategy

  1. Describe visual properties, not the rendering style
  2. Each caption should describe EVERYTHING EXCEPT the concept you’re teaching (the style itself)
  3. Focus on: “warm amber glow,” “Eastern architectural,” “cinematic depth”
  4. Avoid: “anime,” “3D render,” “digital art”

Training Parameters

  1. Start with recommended config, don’t guess hyperparameters
  2. Test intermediate checkpoints (500, 1000, 1500, 2000 steps)
  3. Monitor loss curve: should decrease smoothly without spikes
  4. Use cosine scheduler with warmup (more stable than linear)

Inference Optimization

  1. LoRA strength: 0.6–0.8 for noticeable style
  2. IP-Adapter strength: 0.5–0.7 for reference influence
  3. Steps: 30 sufficient for quality, 40 for polish
  4. CFG: 6.0–7.5 typical; higher (8+) for stronger prompt adherence

Part 6: When to Use Each Approach

Use LoRA if:

  • You’re generating 1000+ images
  • Style must be consistent across different teams/sessions
  • You want permanent, auditable style definition
  • Budget allows 2–4 hour training time

Use IP-Adapter if:

  • Experimenting with multiple style directions
  • Quick iteration/feedback loops required
  • You want non-destructive, instant adjustments
  • One-off projects or proof-of-concepts

Use Hybrid (LoRA + IP-Adapter) if:

  • Production pipeline with consistency requirements
  • Want both fine control and flexibility
  • Can afford modest inference overhead
  • Targeting 90%+ fidelity

Use CLIP Embedding if:

  • Studio-grade output mandatory
  • Team has technical depth for debugging
  • Iterating on very subtle style nuances
  • Cost/latency not primary constraints

Part 7: Recommended Training Command Reference

RunPod Setup

# Clone and install
git clone https://github.com/kohya-ss/sd-scripts.git
cd sd-scripts
pip install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
# Launch GUI
python kohya_gui.py --listen 0.0.0.0 --server_port 7860
# → Access at http://YOUR_RUNPOD_IP:7860

Local CLI Training

python train_network.py \
  --pretrained_model_name_or_path="flux2-pro-gguf-q8.safetensors" \
  --train_data_dir="/path/to/images" \
  --output_dir="/path/to/output" \
  --output_name="style_lora" \
  --network_module="networks.lora_flux" \
  --network_dim=64 \
  --network_alpha=32 \
  --learning_rate=5e-5 \
  --lr_scheduler="cosine_with_restarts" \
  --lr_warmup_steps=100 \
  --max_train_steps=2500 \
  --resolution=1024 \
  --train_batch_size=1 \
  --mixed_precision="bf16" \
  --gradient_checkpointing \
  --cache_latents \
  --caption_extension=".txt"

Additional Resources

  • Kohya_SS Repository: https://github.com/kohya-ss/sd-scripts
  • XLabs IP-Adapter: https://huggingface.co/XLabs-AI/flux-ip-adapter-v2
  • CLIP Vision Model: https://huggingface.co/openai/clip-vit-large-patch14
  • ComfyUI Documentation: https://github.com/comfyanonymous/ComfyUI
  • Apatero Blog (Production guides): Training configurations from real-world projects

title: How to create mute animation similar to YouTube using react native?
excerpt: If you are working on a react native application and would like to have mute animation, here is a simple way to get it done.We will be using “react native reanimated” to get this done. For this, we will be importing useS
html:
If you are working on a react native application and would like to have mute animation, here is a simple way to get it done.

We will be using “react native reanimated” to get this done. For this, we will be importing useSharedvalue, useAnimatedValue, Easing and withTimimg for animation.
In React Native, useSharedValue is a hook provided by the react-native-reanimated library. It’s commonly used for managing animated values in a shared context, particularly in scenarios where multiple animated components need to synchronize their animations.
By the end of this blog, you will achieve animation as shown below. here I have used a microphone icon, you can use a speaker icon similar to YouTube.

Before I get into logic I would like to tell you what we are rendering. To create this animation we need 4 divs which is called as view in react native.
Why do we need 4 div?
first one to create the container(which is optional)
the second one is the touchable opacity which handles the function call/visibility of line.
inside this we will have our icon. after this we will keep our line.

return (
    <View style={styles.container}>
      <TouchableOpacity style={styles.square} onPress={handleClick}>
        <View style={styles.dummyCon}>
        <FontIcon  style={styles.image} name="microphone" size={60} color="grey" />
        <Animated.View style={lineStyle} />
        </View>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );

You can observe in above code that I placed that line(Animated.view) and icon inside a view rather than keeping it as it is? why does that view matter? If we do the animation for that line it won’t work in diagonal direction. it works vertically. You may say why not use transform rotate?
If we use transform rotate CSS, it will change the animation direction in such a way that initial point won’t be fixed. it keeps changing. while you are implementing this you can try doing the animation without using the view and you will spot the difference. This is why placing icon and line inside a view is necessary, so that we can rotate that view and make our animation work without altering anything else.

Logic:

we will write an if-else statement showcasing how much the width and height of the line must be generated on top of the icon to show it has muted.

const handleClick = () => {
    setLineVisible(!lineVisible);
    if (!lineVisible) {
      animatedHeight.value = withTiming(70, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
      animatedWidth.value = withTiming(3, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
    } else {
      animatedHeight.value = withTiming(0, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
      animatedWidth.value = withTiming(0, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
    }
  };j

If the useState of line Visible is false then a line of width 3 and height 70 is generated else it will be 0.
I have used easing from react native to show the smooth animation of line. Easing is a feature extracted from react native reanimated. There are many other options as well you can check out in docs.
When useAnimatedStyle is called, line animation is generated.

const lineStyle = useAnimatedStyle(() => {
    return {
      height: animatedHeight.value,
      width: animatedWidth.value,
      backgroundColor: 'grey',
      position: 'absolute',
      top: -(Math.sqrt(Math.pow(50, 2)*2))/2,
      left: 0,
    };
  });

//linestyle is called using animated view while returning

<Animated.View style={lineStyle} />

I have defined linestyle and called it while rendering the animated view. Here useAnimatedStyle acts as a hook for the animation of line. If we do not use Pythagoras formula in the top, the line animation won’t work from the top left origin point, rather it will start from the middle.

Here is the complete code including styling where you can change the FontIcon as per your needs and use it. you can also refactor the code into props-driven so that it can be used in multiple places.

import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { StyleSheet, View, TouchableOpacity } from 'react-native';
import Animated, {
  useSharedValue,
  withTiming,
  Easing,
  useAnimatedStyle,
} from 'react-native-reanimated';
import FontIcon from 'react-native-vector-icons/FontAwesome';

const Index = () => {
  const [lineVisible, setLineVisible] = useState(false);
  const animatedHeight = useSharedValue(0);
  const animatedWidth = useSharedValue(0);

  const handleClick = () => {
    setLineVisible(!lineVisible);
    if (!lineVisible) {
      animatedHeight.value = withTiming(70, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
      animatedWidth.value = withTiming(3, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
    } else {
      animatedHeight.value = withTiming(0, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
      animatedWidth.value = withTiming(0, {
        duration: 500,
        easing: Easing.inOut(Easing.ease),
      });
    }
  };

  const lineStyle = useAnimatedStyle(() => {
    return {
      height: animatedHeight.value,
      width: animatedWidth.value,
      backgroundColor: 'grey',
      position: 'absolute',
      top: -(Math.sqrt(Math.pow(50, 2)*2))/2,
      left: 0,
    };
  });

  return (
    <View key={2} style={styles.container}>
      <TouchableOpacity style={styles.square} onPress={handleClick}>
        <View style={styles.dummyCon}>
        <FontIcon  style={styles.image} name="microphone" size={60} color="grey" />
        <Animated.View style={lineStyle} />
        </View>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );
};

export default Index;

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  container: {
    flex: 1,
    justifyContent: 'center',
    alignItems: 'center',
    position:"relative",
  },
  square: {
    width: 80,
    height: 80,
    backgroundColor: 'black',
    justifyContent: 'center',
    alignItems: 'center',
    display:"flex",
  },
  dummyCon: {
    transform:[{rotateZ:'-45deg'}],
    display:"flex",
    justifyContent:"center",
    alignItems:"center",
    position:"absolute"
  },
  image:{
    transform:[{rotateZ:'45deg'}],
   position: "absolute",

  }
});

title: Host your website in Firebase easily (Explained all different types of errors you may face)
excerpt: You will learn• setting up Firebase hosting• to fix different types of common errors• Redirect page issueBackstory -We had two websites, one hosted on Firebase, and the other on Vercel. Both had different file directorie
html:
You will learn
• setting up Firebase hosting
• to fix different types of common errors
• Redirect page issue

Backstory -

We had two websites, one hosted on Firebase, and the other on Vercel. Both had different file directories. Our main website needed to be migrated with the content of the second website (hosted on Vercel) without changing the hosting service. While doing this, I faced some issues, and I’ll be explaining everything related to it.

Hosting in firebase

So basically whenever you want to host in Firebase, set up your account and configure it. Later on, install the package and add your email ID. When you run this package, you will be asked to carry out some basic steps related to setting up firebase.

npm i firebase
firebase login

Once you install the package and login to your firebase, you must run the below mentioned command.

firebase init

Navigate using arrow keys to hosting and press the spacebar, then press enter. You will also be asked to define any name for your build folder. Later on, it will ask a question about redirecting to index.html; select “yes”. As for configuring GitHub, it’s up to you. I pressed “no” since I carried out the process manually
Below are the pictures showing the same

Once you are done with firebase init, you need to run the build command.

Web page was not found after hosting

When you run this command all your build files will be created in the folder you named while running firebase init(in my case it is “out” it’s shown in the picture)

npm run build && npm run export

It is important to run the above command as it is the reason for creating all the build files or else you will see the error shown below.
Without the npm run build, if you deploy your website it will look like this, even though it works perfectly fine in localhost.

Even after running that above command, if you get this in your website manually add html.index file in the build folder, it must work.

At the end after completing all the above steps, deploy the site

firebase deploy

App route redirect issue

Consider you have a website that redirects to different pages and sometimes only external links work but the app routing you have set will not work, what I mean is consider there’s a terms and conditions page link you have set in your footer but when you click on it you will be thrown an error saying page not found. Everything works well locally but when you deploy it will face this problem. This happens coz your firebase.json file doesn’t define it to redirect pages.
change your firebase.json file as shown below.

{
  "hosting": {
    "public": "out",
    "ignore": [
      "firebase.json",
      "**/.*",
      "**/node_modules/**"
    ],
    "cleanUrls": true,
    "rewrites": [
      {
        "source": "**",
        "destination": "/index.html"
      }
    ],
    "trailingSlash": true
  }
}

That’s all from this blog. I don’t think you would face any other issues related to firebase. if there are any do comment it, i would like to help you fix it.

title: Sentiment Analysis in detail with an example!
excerpt: Sentiment Analysis in detail with an exampleIn this blog, I will let you know how semantic analysis is actually done by considering a simple Python program example. Before heading to the programming part, let us understa
html:
Sentiment Analysis in detail with an example

In this blog, I will let you know how semantic analysis is actually done by considering a simple Python program example.
Before heading to the programming part, let us understand what sentiment analysis actually does. It Understands and categorizes the opinions, feelings, or attitudes expressed in text data, such as positive, negative, or neutral. Most common example of sentiment analysis is rating reviews such as movies, food, tourist places, etc. Sentiment Analysis can also be used to understand political statements and how they drive public opinion.

Here in our example, we will determine the sentiment analysis for a review of a restaurant. We will import a dataset containing restaurant reviews and determine its sentiment analysis.

This process is done in different steps.

  • Import dataset.
  • clean the dataset
  • Create bag of words model.
  • Split the dataset
  • train the model
  • test the model

Clean the Dataset:

Now, once the dataset is imported, we should clean it. To clean the dataset, we use Porter stemmer and Stopwords.
Porter stemmer is an algorithm used for stemming in English. Stemming is a way of removing suffixes and reducing them to one root word. For example, if there is a review containing the words lovable, loving or loved, it will be reduced to “lov”. Rules are designed to handle common English suffixes, such as “-ing,” “-ed,” “-ly,” and others.
Stopwords are the collection of common words in English that are filtered out. such as articles, conjunctions and prepositions. The purpose of stopwords is to remove unwanted texts are reduce the dimensions. In the end, the main purpose of cleaning the data is to make it accurate and improve the quality. So, stopwords basically consist a list of words and during cleaning, the dataset is compared to the Stopword collection, if the word matches then it is removed. Thus improving the quality of the dataset. These two tools play a vital role in NLP-related tasks.

# Cleaning the texts
import re
import nltk
nltk.download('stopwords')
from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.stem.porter import PorterStemmer
corpus = []
for i in range(0, 1000):
  review = re.sub('[^a-zA-Z]', ' ', dataset['Review'][i])
  review = review.lower()
  review = review.split()
  ps = PorterStemmer()
  all_stopwords = stopwords.words('english')
  all_stopwords.remove('not')
  review = [ps.stem(word) for word in review if not word in set(all_stopwords)]
  review = ' '.join(review)
  corpus.append(review)
print(corpus)

In the above code snippet, you can see we have used English stopwords but excluded the word “not” from the stopword collection because it will mess up our sentiment analysis. For example, “good taste” and “not good taste” . If we won't remove “not” from stopwords, the review will give positive feedback, even though it’s negative. Here we have only removed “not” but it's essential to remove words as many negation words as possible.

Bag of word model:

It’s a commonly used way to represent text data as numerical features for machine learning and statistical analysis. The BoW model treats each document as an unordered collection or “bag” of words, ignoring the structure and grammar of the text. Here’s how the BoW model works:

  1. Tokenization: The first step is to break down the text into individual words or tokens. This process involves removing punctuation and splitting sentences into words.
  2. Vocabulary Creation: A vocabulary is created by compiling a list of unique words (tokens) that appear in the entire corpus of text data.
  3. Word Frequency Count: For each document in the corpus, a vector is created. The length of this vector is equal to the size of the vocabulary. Each element of the vector corresponds to a word in the vocabulary, and its value is the count of how many times that word appears in the document. This step essentially quantifies how frequently each word occurs in each document.
  4. Sparse Representation: Since most documents use only a small subset of the words in the vocabulary, the resulting vectors are typically sparse, meaning that most of the entries are zero. This is because most words in a document don’t appear in that document.
  5. Feature Vectors: The vectors representing each document are used as feature vectors in machine learning or statistical models. These feature vectors can be used for various NLP tasks such as text classification, sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and more.
# Creating the Bag of Words model
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
cv = CountVectorizer(max_features = 1500)
X = cv.fit_transform(corpus).toarray()
y = dataset.iloc[:, -1].values

It uses the CountVectorizer from scikit-learn to convert the text data in the corpus into numerical features. max_features=1500 specifies that only the top 1500 most frequent words will be used as features. The resulting feature matrix is stored in X, and the target variable (positive or negative sentiment) is stored in Y.

Training and testing

# Splitting the dataset into the Training set and Test set
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size = 0.20, random_state = 0)

# Training the Naive Bayes model on the Training set
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
classifier = GaussianNB()
classifier.fit(X_train, y_train)

Here, the dataset is split into training and testing sets using scikit-learn’s train_test_split function. test_size=0.20 specifies that 20% of the data will be used for testing and the rest is used for training, and random_state=0 sets a seed for randomization to ensure reproducibility.
When we call classifier.fit(X_train, y_train), we are essentially telling the model to analyze the data in X_train and learn how the features in X_train relate to the corresponding categories in y_train.

Predicting the test results.

# Predicting the Test set results
y_pred = classifier.predict(X_test)
print(np.concatenate((y_pred.reshape(len(y_pred),1), y_test.reshape(len(y_test),1)),1))

We use classifier to make predictions on a set of input data called X_test. The model takes the features from X_test and tries to predict the corresponding target values
y_pred contains the predicted values generated by the classifier for the test dataset.
y_test contains the actual target values for the test dataset.
y_pred.reshape(len(y_pred), 1) reshapes the y_pred array into a 2D array with one column. This is done to make it compatible with concatenation.
y_test.reshape(len(y_test), 1) does the same reshaping for the y_test array.

Confusion Matrix

A confusion matrix is a table that helps you understand how well your machine learning model is performing in terms of classification. It shows how many of the predicted values match the actual values and how many don’t. It’s useful for problems where you’re trying to classify things into different categories, like whether an email is spam or not spam.

The confusion matrix arranges these values in a table like this:

  [TN FP] -> TN-True Negatives FP-False Positives
  [FN TP] -> FN-False Negatives TP-True Positives

`TN` is in the top-left cell, `FP` in the top-right cell.
`FN` is in the bottom-left cell, and `TP` in the bottom-right cell.

The values in this matrix help you understand how your model is performing. For instance, a good classifier would have a lot of TP and TN and very few FP and FN.

Accuracy Score:
The accuracy score is a simple measure that tells you what proportion of your predictions are correct. It’s calculated by dividing the sum of TP and TN by the sum of all four values (TP + TN + FP + FN).

Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)

# Making the Confusion Matrix
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, accuracy_score
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
print(cm)
accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

So, “cm” will be a table showing how many predictions model got right and wrong for each class. “accuracy_score” will tell the proportion of correct predictions out of all predictions made.

Below is the final code for sentiment analysis for the restaurant.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Importing the dataset
dataset = pd.read_csv('Restaurant_Reviews.tsv', delimiter = '\t', quoting = 3)

# Cleaning the texts
import re
import nltk
nltk.download('stopwords')
from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.stem.porter import PorterStemmer
corpus = []
for i in range(0, 1000):
  review = re.sub('[^a-zA-Z]', ' ', dataset['Review'][i])
  review = review.lower()
  review = review.split()
  ps = PorterStemmer()
  all_stopwords = stopwords.words('english')
  all_stopwords.remove('not')
  review = [ps.stem(word) for word in review if not word in set(all_stopwords)]
  review = ' '.join(review)
  corpus.append(review)
print(corpus)

# Creating the Bag of Words model
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
cv = CountVectorizer(max_features = 1500)
X = cv.fit_transform(corpus).toarray()
y = dataset.iloc[:, -1].values

# Splitting the dataset into the Training set and Test set
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size = 0.20, random_state = 0)

# Training the Naive Bayes model on the Training set
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
classifier = GaussianNB()
classifier.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predicting the Test set results
y_pred = classifier.predict(X_test)
print(np.concatenate((y_pred.reshape(len(y_pred),1), y_test.reshape(len(y_test),1)),1))

# Making the Confusion Matrix
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, accuracy_score
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
print(cm)
accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

How to authenticate users?

In this blog, I’ll give you a detailed breakdown of authentication. Authentication is basically verifying the user and letting him use your platform, right?

So, this authentication can be done in different ways based on how secure they are. There are six levels of authentication, each level with a different type of security.

Now, first I’ll give an overview of all these types of authentication and later on, we will go through each one of them.

  • Level 1 is just letting the user register by giving a username and password which is stored in MongoDB database. This cannot be used in a production environment.
  • Level 2 is encrypting the password and storing it in database. This makes the password secure in case database is breached.
  • Level 3 is hashing the password. encrypted passwords can be decrypted but whereas hashed passwords are almost impossible to crack.
  • Level 4 is salting and hashing the password, salting plays a vital role since hash will be the same if words are repetitive. salting solves this.
  • Level 5 is using passport js with cookies and sessions. Here data will be saved until user logs out, basically, user can leave with half work and the website will store it until he logs out.
  • And finally, the most important one, level 6 is OAuth. This makes the whole process easy by letting the user login through any other organizations such as Google, Facebook, etc.

To get through each one of these we need to have app.js file with basic server connection ready. Also a form for username and password with login and register buttons.


const express = require('express');
const ejs = require('ejs');
const bodyParser = require('body-parser');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const app = express();


app.use(express.static( "public"));
app.set('view engine', 'ejs');
app.use(bodyParser.urlencoded({extended: true}));

app.get("/", (req,res) => {
    res.render("home");
});

app.get("/login", (req,res) => {
    res.render("login");
});

app.get("/register", (req,res) => {
    res.render("register");
});





app.listen(3000, () => {
    console.log("server is running");
    });

Here in this above code, you can see we have different dependencies and routing ready to render home, login, and register. Now, our server connection is ready and we can start manipulating this file for implementing different authentication process.

Level 1: username and password using database.

This is the simplest way to authenticate but this is no were used and not at all secure. Here we simply connect it to MongoDB and store the username and password.
Now, you can see in code there are two app.post. we use app.post to grab the username and password, further using newUser.save we save it and use another app.post for login we use User.findOne to let the user login our page by checking whether he has already registered or not.

const express = require('express');
const ejs = require('ejs');
const bodyParser = require('body-parser');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const app = express();

const MONGO_URI = '';

app.use(express.static("public"));
app.set('view engine', 'ejs');
app.use(bodyParser.urlencoded({ extended: true }));

mongoose.connect(MONGO_URI, { useNewUrlParser: true, useUnifiedTopology: true })
  .then(() => console.log('Database connected'))
  .catch(err => console.error(err));

const dataSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  user: String,
  password: String,
});

const Data = mongoose.model('Data', dataSchema);

app.get("/", (req, res) => {
  res.render("home");
});

app.get("/login", (req, res) => {
  res.render("login");
});

app.get("/register", (req, res) => {
  res.render("register");
});

app.post('/register', (req, res) => {
  const username = req.body;

  const newUser = new Data({
    user: username,
    password: password,
  });

  newUser.save()
    .then(() => res.render("blogs"))
    .catch(err => {
      console.error(err);
      res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
    });
});

app.post("/login", (req, res) => {
  const { username, password } = req.body;

  Data.findOne({ user: username })
    .then(foundUser => {
      if (foundUser) {
        if (foundUser.password === password) {
          return res.render("blogs");
        } else {
          return res.status(401).send("Unauthorized");
        }
      } else {
        return res.status(404).send("Invalid username or password");
      }
    })
    .catch(error => {
      console.error(error);
      res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
    });
});

app.listen(3001, () => {
  console.log("server is running");
});

Level 2: Password encryption.

As we know level 1 authentication is not at all secure, now here we encrypt passwords so that even after hackers get into our database, passwords will be secure. To do this we must install mongoose-encryption.

step 1: run “npm i mongoose-encryption” command in your project terminal.
Also, add const mongooseEncryption = require(‘mongoose-encryption’)

step 2: add the plugin which is shown below.
Below our schema, create your secret phrase and encrypt it. You can do it as mentioned below. It is advisable to keep the secret phrase in .dotenv file rather than app.js so that it won’t be exposed while uploading in Github.

const secret : "iAmBatmanBelieveMe";
userSchema.plugin(encrypt, {blog: blog, encryptedFields: ['password'] });

finally, app.js file must look something like this.

Level 3: Hashing Passwords

Hashing passwords are lot more secure than any sort of encryption coz anything which is encrypted can be decrypted whereas hashing is almost impossible to crack it.
There are different hashing algorithms such as md5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512. Here, we will use md5 algorithm. As usual first step is to install the package and require it.
To get started first we need to remove mongoose-encryption package and the plugin which we added in the last method. Now, run npm i md5 in terminal of your project directory and add const md5 = require(‘md5’)
When a user registers, we don’t want to save their passwords. So, we change req.body.password by adding it within brackets of md5. Something like this.

app.post('/register', (req,res)=> {
   
    const newUser = new Data({
        user: req.body.username,
        password : md5(req.body.password)
        
});

same thing must be done for login post route, here also do the same changes for password .

app.post('/login', (req,res)=> {
   const username = req.body.username; 
    const password = md5(req.body.password);
  Data.findOne(
.....
.....
....
        
});

Thats it. with these changes, you can successfully use hashing technique to secure passwords but there is one problem with this if the words are same, the hash will also be the same. what I mean is, if a lot of users are using the same general repetitive passwords then they can be identified based on patterns. In past many repetitive passwords have been released and we know hash for that.
But, there is a solution for this, we use something called salting. we will see salting in the next method.

Level 4: Salting and Hashing

To carry out this process we need a package called bcrypt.
Using this package we will carry salt rounds as well. Salt rounds are the number of times you salt your password. More the salting rounds, more consumption of time. It is ideal to carry out 10–15 salt rounds, that is what we will do here.
first, install bcrypt and require it. As well as define the number of salt rounds.

const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');
const saltRounds = 10;

Now, change post route of register as follows. You can find bcrypt.hash code in bcrypt npm docs. These lines of code carry out the salt rounds and create hash. Remove all md5 related code so that you won’t get any errors while running the file.


app.post('/register', (req,res)=> {
   bcrypt.hash(myPlaintextPassword, saltRounds, function(err, hash) {
    const newUser = new Data({

        password : md5('req.body.password')
        
});
newUser.save()
    .then(() => res.render("secrets"))
    .catch(err => {
      console.error(err);
      res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
    });
}
});

We should also change login route by using bcrypt.compare, we will check whether the users has already registered and his hash is stored in database. you can find bcrypt.compare method in bcrypt npm docs. Here we have replaced myPlaintextPassword to password and hash is replaced to foundUser.password since hash is stored inside foundUser.


app.post("/login", async (req, res) => {

    const password = bcrypt('req.body.password');

    try {
        const foundUser = await User.findOne({ email: username });

        if (foundUser && (await bcrypt.compare(password, foundUser.password))) {

Level 5: using passport js

Now, as i mentioned starting of the blog, here we use cookies and session. why do we need to do it? why can’t we just use level 4 and not create any cookies. This is important coz, whenever user login to a platform, he does certain activites and he may leave it half done. So, basically creating cookies will let the user have it stored until session doesn’t end. A session is created when user login and session ends when he logs out.

As always first thing is installing packages, here we need a bunch of packages.
npm i passport
npm i passport-local
npm i passport-local-mongoose
npm i express-session
Once we add these packages, we will require it as we did previously.

Now we need to call a session, so as mentioned in express-session docs, we copy that code and edit it. This code must be placed above mongoose connect and below app.use and app.set. Next, we will tell our app to use passport to initialize and start session using passport package. This whole thing is shown below, make sure to check out passport docs to understand more about this.


app.use(session({
  secret: 'iAmBatmanBelieveMe',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: true,

}));

app.use(passport.initialize());
app.use(passport.session());

Now we will add plugin, as you remember we added a plugin during password encryption. Here we do something similar, now we will set up passport local mongoose as a plugin. This bit of code must be written after Mongoose connection, make sure you place it right.

userSchema.plugin(passportLocalMongoose);

Now we need to configure passport local mongoose, for this, we should paste three lines of code from passport-local-mongoose docs below the mongoose model.

passport.use(User.createStrategy());

passport.serializeUser(User.serializeUser());
passport.deserializeUser(User.deserializeUser());

Next, we need to set up register and login post routes.
To do this, first clear everything we wrote inside app.post route, when we look into passport-local-mongoose documentation, they have shown how to register any user in examples. we should implement user.register and give username and password then we will see if the user has already registered or not and redirect him to the desired page.
If the authentication is successful, we will let the users enter the blogs section. Previously if you observe we never had any blogs route because we always let them enter through login or register route but here we are using password.authenticate and the user will be in the session until he logs out, we need to create a separate route for blogs. Once you implement configuration and routing without any errors, you can observe that when a user registers to your project, a session would be created, this you can verify it in your browser.
Similar process is used to route login, here first we must create the user later on as mentioned in passport docs, we will use req.login to check whether user already exists or not and redirect him to blogs page. we use passport.authenticate which helps in verifying the user and creating the session.

    red.redirect("/blogs");
  });
} catch (err) {
  console.log(err);
}
)};

app.listen(3001, () => {
    console.log("server is running");
    });

Level 6: Oauth 2.0 — open authorization

Basically, open authorization is a way of letting the user login without creating a new account. He will just pass in using his Google account. Open authorization is not only limited to Google, you can do it using Facebook, GitHub, LinkedIn, etc. But here we will be going through Google account. To implement this, it’s very important to look at the docs of passport Google OAuth, it is clearly mentioned we need to register our project in the google cloud console.
So head over to google console and create a new project.

  • Once new project is created, you need to create OAuth client ID.
  • credentials > create OAuth client ID
  • if you are working in local system, add the localhost URL in javascript origin
  • In authorized redirect URIs section add the following link as shown below. here I have used “blogs” at the end coz I want o redirect to that route, choose it based on your requirements.

Once you are done with setting up client ID. Add “passport-google-oauth20” npm package in your project directory. Also, paste client ID and client secret in .env file.
we need to add client id and secret details below serialize and deserialize. As you observed while doing passport js we created serialize and deserialize. This particular code snippet mentioned below is written in passport google OAuth docs. The access token allows us to get data related to that user. It is important to note that findOrCreate is not a function. so we should install a package called mongoose findOrCreate. we also need to add a plugin findOrCreate. There is one more important thing to do, we only have routes for login and register but here we are doing it directly through google account. So, our frontend code for connecting with google account doesn’t have app.get in the backend to authenticate it, it’s important to create one. we create one more get function to redirect to the desired page once user is authenticated. So, basically, we need to create two.
All the extra code we needed to add is shown below.

passport.use(new GoogleStrategy({
    clientID: process.env.CLIENT_ID,
    clientSecret: process.env.CLIENT_SECRET,
    callbackURL: "http://localhost:3000/auth/google/blogs",
    userProfileURL: "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/userinfo"
}, async (accessToken, refreshToken, profile, cb) => {
  console.log(profile);
  
  try {
    const existingUser = await User.findOne({ googleId: profile.id });

    if (existingUser) {
      return cb(null, existingUser);
    } else {
      const newUser = new User({
        googleId: profile.id,
        email: profile.emails[0].value // Assuming the email is stored in the first element of the emails array
      });

      await newUser.save();
      return cb(null, newUser);
    }
  } catch (err) {
    return cb(err, null);
  }
}));

.

userSchema.plugin('findOrCreate');

.

.

app.get("/auth/google",
  passport.authenticate('google', { scope: ["profile"] })
);

app.get("/auth/google/blogs",
  passport.authenticate('google', { failureRedirect: "/login" }),
  function (req, res) {
    res.redirect("/blogs");

final app.js file

require('dotenv').config();
const express = require("express");
const bodyParser = require("body-parser");
const ejs = require("ejs");
const mongoose = require("mongoose");
const session = require('express-session');
const passport = require("passport");
const passportLocalMongoose = require("passport-local-mongoose");
const GoogleStrategy = require('passport-google-oauth20').Strategy;
const findOrCreate = require('mongoose-findorcreate');

const app = express();

const MONGO_URI = '';

app.use(express.static("public"));
app.set('view engine', 'ejs');
app.use(bodyParser.urlencoded({ extended: true }));

app.use(session({
  secret: "Our little secret.",
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false
}));

app.use(passport.initialize());
app.use(passport.session());

async function connectToDatabase() {
  try {
    await mongoose.connect(MONGO_URI, { useNewUrlParser: true, useUnifiedTopology: true });
    console.log('Database connected');
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}

connectToDatabase();

const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  email: String,
  password: String,
  googleId: String,
  secret: String
});

userSchema.plugin(passportLocalMongoose);
userSchema.plugin(findOrCreate);

const User = new mongoose.model("User", userSchema);

passport.use(User.createStrategy());
passport.serializeUser(function (user, done) {
  done(null, user.id); // Serialize only the user id
});
passport.deserializeUser(async function (id, done) {
  try {
    const user = await User.findById(id);
    done(null, user);
  } catch (err) {
    done(err, null);
  }
});


passport.use(new GoogleStrategy({
    clientID: process.env.CLIENT_ID,
    clientSecret: process.env.CLIENT_SECRET,
    callbackURL: "http://localhost:3000/auth/google/blogs",
    userProfileURL: "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/userinfo"
}, async (accessToken, refreshToken, profile, cb) => {
  console.log(profile);
  
  try {
    const existingUser = await User.findOne({ googleId: profile.id });

    if (existingUser) {
      return cb(null, existingUser);
    } else {
      const newUser = new User({
        googleId: profile.id,
        email: profile.emails[0].value // Assuming the email is stored in the first element of the emails array
      });

      await newUser.save();
      return cb(null, newUser);
    }
  } catch (err) {
    return cb(err, null);
  }
}));



app.get("/", function (req, res) {
  res.render("home");
});

app.get("/auth/google",
  passport.authenticate('google', { scope: ["profile"] })
);

app.get("/auth/google/blogs",
  passport.authenticate('google', { failureRedirect: "/login" }),
  function (req, res) {
    res.redirect("/blogs");
  }
);

app.get("/login", function (req, res) {
  res.render("login");
});

app.get("/register", function (req, res) {
  res.render("register");
});

app.get("/blogs", async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const foundUsers = await User.find({ "blog": { $ne: null } });
    res.render("blogs", { usersWithBlogs: foundUsers });
  } catch (err) {
    console.log(err);
    res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
  }
});


app.post("/register", async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const user = await User.register({ username: req.body.username }, req.body.password);
    req.login(user, function (err) {
      if (err) {
        console.log(err);
        res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
      } else {
        res.redirect("/blogs");
      }
    });
  } catch (err) {
    console.log(err);
    res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
  }
});

app.post("/login", async (req, res) => {
  const user = new User({
    username: req.body.username,
    password: req.body.password
  });

  try {
    await req.login(user);
    res.redirect("/blogs");
  } catch (err) {
    console.log(err);
    res.status(500).send("Internal Server Error");
  }
});

app.listen(3000, function () {
  console.log("Server started on port 3000.");
});




You now have the understanding of authenticating the users with any of these methods but adding an OAuth option will let the users log in easily so that they do not need to spend more time creating an account. Messing around with OAuth is important since it is ultimately the most essential thing to know.

title: Events in Solidity: Create Interactive Smart Contracts
excerpt: Solidity events: Create Interactive Smart ContractsToday we will explore how smart contracts can communicate and interact with the world around them. In this blog post, we’ll dive into the world of events, a key feature
html:
Solidity events: Create Interactive Smart Contracts

Today we will explore how smart contracts can communicate and interact with the world around them. In this blog post, we’ll dive into the world of events, a key feature of Solidity, and discover how they bring life and interactivity to decentralized applications.

Imagine your smart contract as a wizard casting spells in a magical world. Events are like signals that the wizard sends out to let others know when something important happens. These events act as messengers, notifying external systems or other contracts about specific occurrences or changes in the contract’s state. Events allow smart contracts to emit signals or notifications about specific occurrences or changes in the contract’s state. They serve as a way to communicate between smart contracts and off-chain applications, enabling decentralized applications (dApps) to interact with users, wallets, or other smart contracts.

Creating Events:

To use events in Solidity, we need to declare them inside our smart contracts. It’s like creating a blueprint for the type of message we want to send. We give the event a name and define the information we want to include with the event. For example, if we want to notify others about a transfer of tokens, we can declare an event called “Transfer” and include the sender’s address, the recipient’s address, and the amount transferred.

event Transfer(address indexed from, address indexed to, uint256 value);

Triggering Events:

Once we have our events declared, we can trigger them when something important happens in our smart contract. It’s like raising a flag to let everyone know that an event has occurred. We use the “emit” keyword followed by the event name and provide the necessary information. This emits the event and notifies external systems or other contracts that something has happened.

emit Transfer(msg.sender, recipient, amount);

Listening to Events:

Now that our events are emitted, it’s time for other applications or contracts to listen and respond. Think of it like tuning in to a radio station to catch the latest news. External systems can listen to specific events emitted by our smart contract and take action based on those events. This allows for dynamic interactions between the smart contract and the world outside. Off-chain applications, such as web interfaces or other smart contracts, can listen to emitted events using various methods provided by web3 libraries or Ethereum client APIs. These methods enable applications to be notified whenever an event is emitted. Event listeners typically specify the contract address and the event they want to listen to

Event Logs:

Solidity automatically keeps a log of all the events emitted by a smart contract. These logs serve as a historical record, like a diary of past events. They can be useful for auditing and understanding the contract’s behavior over time. Developers can access these logs programmatically and analyze them to gain insights. These logs are stored in the blockchain and are useful for auditing and querying past events. You can access event logs programmatically using the contract’s address, event name, and any filter criteria. Solidity provides the event object to access event data inside the contract itself. It allows contracts to inspect the emitted events, facilitating conditional execution based on event occurrence.

Conclusion:

Event-driven programming in Solidity opens up a world of possibilities for creating interactive and responsive decentralized applications. By using events, smart contracts can communicate with external systems, notify others about important occurrences, and trigger actions based on those events. So, whether you’re building a game, a financial application, or something entirely new, harness the power of events to create a dynamic and engaging experience.
Remember, events are the language that allows your smart contracts to speak to the world, making them an essential tool in your Solidity programming toolkit. Happy coding and exploring the exciting world of event-driven programming in Solidity!

Now, try answering these questions

  1. What happens if an event is emitted inside a loop that iterates a large number of times?
  2. How can you ensure the order of events emitted by a contract is maintained when retrieving them programmatically?
  3. Can events carry complex data structures, such as arrays or structs, as parameters?
  4. Is it possible to update or modify an emitted event after it has been emitted? Why or why not?

Press that follow button for more blogs!
peace out

title: The Game-Changing Fusion of Spatial Analytics with AI!
excerpt: We had a seminar few days ago where the speaker talked about spatial analytics. Since it was an interesting and wonderful session, I would let you know how spatial analytics has been in our lives for so many years even t
html:
We had a seminar few days ago where the speaker talked about spatial analytics. Since it was an interesting and wonderful session, I would let you know how spatial analytics has been in our lives for so many years even though we never observed it. By the end of the blog, you will get a clear understanding of what is spatial analytics and its use case as well as how to build applications based on spatial analytics.

Spatial analytics is kinda data analytics, here we analyze the information related to topology, location, or geometrics. That is, there will be a detailed study done on the geography of the location to analyze the situation to solve the problem.

Some of the real examples of spatial analytics:

In Africa, poaching has been a matter of concern for several years, but recently poaching has decreased due to spatial analytics. similarly, when cholera was hit badly, it was difficult to identify how random people are getting this disease, when spatial analytics was done later on it was discovered that one particular area of people have been hit by cholera and the main reason was the borewell water.

When covid broke out, all the analytics on the number of people being infected and all the people who died were easily tracked due to spatial analytics. Now we have all the information on where and in which part of the world, how many people had covid.

How spatial analytics is done:

Spatial analytics is the process of analyzing data that has a geographic or spatial component. This involves the use of geographic information systems (GIS) software and other tools to manipulate and analyze data.

To carry out successful spatial analytics it involves data collection, data preparation, data analysis, and visualization. Clustering and interpolation technics are used to do effective spatial analytics that is identifying areas where the data is available in bulk and also check on the location where there is no data.

Spatial Analytics and AI:

The interesting thing about spatial analytics is that you can use the data collected to predict the outcomes or understand the market better, One of the key benefits of spatial analytics in AI is its ability to improve decision-making. By using spatial data, organizations can make more informed choices that are backed by real-world insights. For example, a retailer may use spatial analytics to identify the optimal location for a new store based on demographic data, traffic patterns, and other spatial data points.

Consider you have planned to open a restaurant in Bangalore, now you have data of all the restaurants that are in Bangalore with details of cuisine and prices they are selling, you can use this spatial data to analyze which is the optimal place to open the restaurant, where there is the crowd and what tier people live there. By using this you can decide the place of your restaurant. The same goes for real estate as well.

Spatial analytics can also be used to optimize operations and improve efficiency. For example, a logistics company may use spatial analytics to optimize delivery routes, reducing travel time and fuel costs. Similarly, a utility company may use spatial analytics to identify areas where power outages are likely to occur, enabling them to proactively deploy resources to prevent outages from occurring.

Let me give some real-life practical projects that are done using spatial analytics: Traffic analysis tools, food delivery apps, crime mapping applications and many more.

How to build a crime mapping application?

  • Gather the data: It is important to have all the information on the number of crimes that occurred in a particular location in the past 10–15 years. The more the data, the better will be the results.
  • Convert the address: When all the data is gathered and prepared for analysis, the next step is to geocode the addresses. What it actually means is, convert the normal address into latitude and longitude coordinates. You can use geocoding tools like Google Maps API to geocode the addresses.
  • Create a crime map: Once geocode is ready, create a crime map using mapping tools like QGIS or Leaflet. Analyze the crime data and create a map that shows the locations of crimes, the type of crime, and the frequency of incidents. You can also add other layers to the map, such as the location of police stations or the boundaries of different neighborhoods, etc.
  • Deploy the application: Finally, you can deploy the crime mapping application on a website or a mobile app. Users can access the app to find out the crime rate in their neighborhood and take appropriate safety measures.

I found an application built by Vikram Bhati which predicts the crimes in the Indore city.

Here is the link for the code file https://github.com/vikram-bhati/PAASBAAN-crime-prediction

title: Solidity cryptographic functions
excerpt: Smart contracts often handle sensitive data and transactions, which require high level of security and privacy. Cryptography is a critical aspect of Solidity programming as it provides a way to secure the data and transa
html:
Smart contracts often handle sensitive data and transactions, which require high level of security and privacy. Cryptography is a critical aspect of Solidity programming as it provides a way to secure the data and transactions that are stored and processed in smart contracts.

One of the most commonly used cryptographic functions in Solidity is encryption and decryption. Encryption is the process of converting plaintext into ciphertext using an algorithm and a secret key, while decryption is the reverse process of converting ciphertext back into plaintext using the same key and algorithm. In Solidity, symmetric key encryption and asymmetric key encryption are both used depending on the use case.

Symmetric Key Encryption

Symmetric key encryption is a method in which the same key is used for both encryption and decryption. This type of encryption is useful for securing communication between two parties who share a secret key. However, the main challenge with symmetric key encryption is key distribution, which can be difficult to manage for large groups of users.

Asymmetric Key Encryption

Asymmetric key encryption is a method in which two different keys are used for encryption and decryption. The public key is available to anyone, while the private key is kept secret. Asymmetric key encryption is useful for secure communication between multiple parties since each party can have its own public and private keys. The most popular asymmetric key encryption algorithm is the RSA algorithm.

Hash functions in solidity

Hash functions are commonly used for password storage and many other applications. When a user creates a password, for example, the password is often hashed and the hash value is stored in a database. When the user enters the password to authenticate, the input password is hashed again and compared with the stored hash value. If the two hashes match, the user is granted access.

In Solidity, the most commonly used hash function is keccak256, which is based on the Keccak-256 hash function. The syntax for using keccak256 in Solidity is as follows:

bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(input));

Here is an example to convert a data into hash:

function hash(string memory data) public pure returns (bytes32) {
    return keccak256(abi.encode(data));
}

Signature verifications:

Signature verification functions are used to verify the authenticity of a digital signature. Solidity provides several signature verification functions, including ecrecover and recover.

signature verification in solidity is done in 4 steps.

  • the message which needs to be signed
  • hash the message
  • sign the message (It is done off-chain)
  • ecrecover (pass the hash of the message and signature it then returns the sign)

ecrecover is a special function that can be used to check if a digital signature is real or not. Think of it like checking if someone's signature on a piece of paper is real or not. But in the digital world, we need a special tool to check digital signatures, and that's where ecrecover comes in.

When someone wants to sign a message digitally, they use a special algorithm to create a “signature” that is unique to that message. This signature is like a secret code that only that person can create. Then they send the message and the signature to someone else, like a website or a smart contract.

When the website or smart contract receives the message and signature, it can use ecrecover to check if the signature is real or not. ecrecover does this by using some complicated math to figure out if the signature matches the message and was created by the person who claims to have created it.

If the signature is real, ecrecover will return the address of the person who signed the message. If the signature is fake, ecrecover won't return anything, which means the signature is invalid.

So basically, it is a tool that can be used to check if a digital signature is real or fake, and it does this using some complicated math to figure out if the signature matches the message and was created by the right person.

ecrecover takes four parameters:

  1. hash - the hash of the message that was signed
  2. v - the recovery ID (ECDSA parameter)
  3. r - the R-value of the signature (ECDSA parameter)
  4. s - the S value of the signature (ECDSA parameter)

Here is an basic example of how ecrecover can be used in a Solidity function:

pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract SignatureVerifier {
    function verifySignature(bytes32 message, uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s, address signer) public pure returns (bool) {
        bytes32 prefixedHash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19Ethereum Signed Message:\n32", message));
        address recoveredAddress = ecrecover(prefixedHash, v, r, s);
        if (recoveredAddress == signer) {
            return true;
        } else {
            return false;
        }
    }
}

In this example, the verifySignature function takes four parameters: the message hash, the recovery ID v, and the R and S values of the signature, as well as the Ethereum address of the signer. The function first calculates the prefixed hash using the keccak256 function, then uses the ecrecover function to recover the Ethereum address of the signer. The recovered address is then compared to the signer parameter to check if it matches.

If the recovered address matches the signer parameter, the function returns true, which means the signature is valid. If the recovered address doesn't match the signer parameter, the function returns false, which means the signature is invalid.

Note that this example function is a simplified example and doesn’t include all the necessary checks and validation that should be performed in a production-grade smart contract. Additionally, the gas cost of using ecrecover can be relatively high, so it should be used carefully and sparingly.

title: Blockchain and counterfeit drugs .
excerpt: In 2017 a WHO report was published claiming 10.5 percent of medicines sold in developing countries including India are fake. In 2020 counterfeit medications were the seventh most commonly seized product, whereas in 2019
html:
In 2017 a WHO report was published claiming 10.5 percent of medicines sold in developing countries including India are fake. In 2020 counterfeit medications were the seventh most commonly seized product, whereas in 2019 they were tenth.

The most common problem in India is that there is barely any authentic platform to verify the genuiness of the product.

Blockchain will help build a track and trace system for the supply chain of products.

Here is how it works:

A distributed ledger provides an immutable and secure record-keeping system that can record every transaction within the supply chain, from the manufacturer to the end consumer. Each transaction is verified and recorded in a secure and tamper-proof manner.

All the data must be added to the ledger, such as the origin, manufacturing date, lot numbers and other relevant information about the drugs. Smart contracts are used to automate the process of verifying the authenticity of drugs.

Since it is a decentralized system, there won’t be any control or authority. This makes it difficult for counterfeiters to manipulate or interfere with the supply chain, reducing the risk of counterfeit drugs entering the system.

The most essential part is having QR codes. This must be added to drug packaging, which can be scanned by consumers to verify the authenticity of the drug. The QR code can be linked to the blockchain record, providing real-time access to the entire supply chain history of the drug.

Currently NITI Aayog is using this concept to fight counterfeit drugs.

title: Solidity function to implement a trustless escrow system, where funds are held in escrow until…
excerpt: Solidity function to implement a trustless escrow system, where funds are held in escrow until certain conditions are met.Before we dive into code, let us know what is escrow. In simple words, escrow is a third party tha
html:
Solidity function to implement a trustless escrow system, where funds are held in escrow until certain conditions are met.

Before we dive into code, let us know what is escrow. In simple words, escrow is a third party that manages money between any two clients. This collects all the funds and locks them until all the required agreements and document process is complete. Once it is done, the funds are released to the desired client. This helps to achieve a trustless contract to complete any sort of business.

Real estates and business transactions are the popular use cases of escrow. So, now we know what escrow is, let us understand how to write a smart contract that locks and releases the funds. Since we know there are 3 people in the whole process. so we have 3 main variables that is buyer, seller, and mediator, also the last one is the amount. // Now there are certain conditions that need to be considered, so we use modifiers to accomplish those. the conditions are only the buyer should send the money, similarly only the seller and only the mediator should be part, and no other address must be involved. Once the modifiers are designed, we use functions to lock, release, refund, and resolve the money.

Let us write the complete smart contract on the escrow system using solidity.

pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract escrow {

    address payable public buyer;
    address payable public seller;
    address payable public mediator;
    uint amount;

    enum situation {Active, Locked, Released,  Inactive} 
        situation public state;
    
    constructor(address payable _buyer, address payable _seller, address payable _mediator, uint _amount ){

        buyer = _buyer;
        seller = _seller;
        mediator = _mediator;
        amount = _amount;
        //  state = situation.Active;
}

modifier currentSituation(situation _state){
    require(state == _state, "inavlid state");
    _;
}

modifier onlyBuyer{
    require(msg.sender == buyer, "money is not send buy the buyer");
    _;
}

modifier onlySeller{
    require(msg.sender == seller, "money is not sent to the seller");
    _;
}

modifier onlyMediator{
    require(msg.sender == mediator, "money is not in the hands of mediator");
    _;
}

function lock() payable public currentSituation(situation.Active) onlyBuyer {
    require(msg.value == amount, "amount is not met as per escrow");
    state = situation.Locked;
  
}

function release() public currentSituation(situation.Locked) onlySeller{
    seller.transfer(amount); //important step
    state = situation.Released;
}

function refund() public currentSituation(situation.Locked) onlyMediator{
    buyer.transfer(amount);
    state = situation.Inactive ; 
}

 function resolve() public currentSituation(situation.Locked) onlyMediator{
        seller.transfer(amount / 2);
        buyer.transfer(amount / 2);
        state = situation.Inactive;
    }

function getSituation() public view returns(uint){
    return amount;
}

function getBalance() public view returns(uint){
    return address(this).balance; //imp step
}

}
